section.title: Lawsuits Among Believers
section.sectionRationale: The proud, divided Corinthians were dragging one another before pagan courts, airing their disputes before unbelievers. Paul is astonished: the saints will one day judge the world and even angels, so they are surely competent to settle trivial matters among themselves. To have lawsuits at all is already a defeat; better to be wronged than to shame the church and the name of Christ. Paul then presses further, warning that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God, and reminding the Corinthians of the great reversal of the gospel: such were some of you, but you were washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit.
section.thesis: Believers who take one another to court before unbelievers shame the church and are already defeated, for the saints will judge the world and angels and ought rather to be wronged than to sin against a brother; yet the deepest hope is that those once marked by such sins have been washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the scandal of lawsuits before unbelievers, when the saints will judge the world and angels; the call rather to be wronged than to wrong a brother; and the sober warning that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom, answered by the gospel reversal, but you were washed, sanctified, justified.

units.label: Lawsuits before unbelievers
units.note: the saints will judge the world and angels

verses.text: If any of you has a grievance against another, how dare he go to law before the {{adikos|unrighteous}} instead of before the saints!
Do you not know that the saints will {{krino|judge}} the world? And if you are to judge the world, are you not competent to judge trivial cases?
Do you not know that we will judge angels? How much more the things of this life!
So if you need to settle everyday matters, do you appoint as judges those of no standing in the church?
I say this to your shame. Is there really no one among you wise enough to arbitrate between his brothers?
Instead, one brother goes to law against another, and this in front of unbelievers!

units.label: Better to be wronged
units.note: the lawsuits themselves are already a defeat

verses.text: The very fact that you have lawsuits among you means that you are thoroughly defeated already. Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be cheated?
Instead, you yourselves cheat and do wrong, even against your own brothers!

units.label: Washed, sanctified, justified
units.note: the unrighteous will not inherit, but such were some of you

verses.text: Do you not know that the wicked will not {{kleronomeo|inherit}} the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived: Neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor men who submit to or perform homosexual acts,
nor thieves, nor the greedy, nor drunkards, nor verbal abusers, nor swindlers, will inherit the kingdom of God.
And that is what some of you were. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were {{dikaioo|justified}}, in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God.

adikos.gloss: Unrighteous, unjust, one who does not do what is right. Paul uses it for the pagan judges before whom the Corinthians were dragging their disputes, and it forms a pointed link with the unrighteous who will not inherit the kingdom in verse 9. It is folly to seek justice from the unrighteous when the saints themselves are destined to judge the world.

krino.gloss: To judge, to decide, to pass verdict. Paul reminds the saints of their astonishing future: they will judge the world and even angels alongside Christ. If such judgment awaits them, they are surely able to arbitrate the small everyday matters that divide them now, rather than parading their disputes before unbelieving courts.

kleronomeo.gloss: To inherit, to receive a share in an inheritance as an heir. The kingdom of God is an inheritance, received not earned, yet those who persist unrepentant in the works of the flesh show they have no part in it. The warning is solemn and real, and it sets up the glorious contrast of verse 11, that such people can be washed and made heirs.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare righteous, to acquit and count as right before God. Paul says the Corinthians, once marked by the sins that bar people from the kingdom, were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit. It is God's verdict of righteousness, grounded in Christ, that transfers them from the ranks of the unrighteous to the heirs of the kingdom.

basic.context: The Corinthians were suing one another in the pagan courts, letting unbelievers settle disputes between believers. Paul is amazed. Do they not know that one day the saints will judge the world, and even angels? If so, they can surely handle small everyday matters among themselves. In fact, having lawsuits at all is already a defeat; it would be better to accept being wronged than to shame Christ before outsiders. Yet instead they were the ones cheating their own brothers. Paul then warns that people who live in unrepentant sin will not inherit God's kingdom, and lists the very sins that describe the old life. But then comes the turn of the gospel: such were some of you. You were washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul argues from the believers' future glory, judging the world and angels, back to their present pettiness. The vice list in verses 9 and 10 is a sober warning, not a cold catalogue; it exists to magnify the grace of verse 11. The threefold washed, sanctified, justified is the heart of the passage and must be read as the definitive answer to the warning.
basic.originalLanguages: It is folly to seek justice from the adikos, the unrighteous, when the saints will one day krino, judge, the world and angels. Paul warns that the unrighteous will not kleronomeo, inherit, the kingdom, an inheritance received by grace. Then the great reversal: the Corinthians were dikaioo, justified, declared righteous, in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit.
basic.typology: The saints reigning and judging with Christ fulfil the promise that God's people would share in His rule, seen in the vision of the holy ones given judgment in Daniel and echoed in the reign of the saints in Revelation.
basic.christ: The transformation of the Corinthians happens in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ: His person and work are the ground of their washing, sanctifying, and justifying. Christ is the judge with whom the saints will judge the world, and it is union with Him, applied by the Spirit, that moves people from the list of the unrighteous to the heirs of the kingdom. The willingness to be wronged rather than retaliate reflects the pattern of Christ, who when reviled did not revile in return.
basic.god: God is just and will judge; the unrighteous will not inherit His kingdom. Yet God is the one who washes, sanctifies, and justifies sinners, taking the initiative to make the unrighteous righteous through Christ and by His Spirit. The kingdom is His to give as an inheritance, and He gives it freely to those who are His in Christ. His justice and His mercy are both fully in view.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 7 promises judgment given to the saints of the Most High. Revelation 20 shows the saints reigning and judging with Christ. Romans 12 urges believers not to avenge themselves but to leave room for God's justice. Titus 3 describes the washing of regeneration and renewal by the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: God is forming a people who will share in His righteous rule, judging the world and angels with Christ. The petty lawsuits of Corinth deny that destiny. But the gospel reversal, washed, sanctified, justified, is the story of the whole Bible in miniature: God takes the unrighteous and makes them heirs of His kingdom through Christ and by the Spirit. The story runs from sinners barred from the kingdom to saints who will judge the world.

context.literary: The chapter continues Paul's response to reports of Corinthian disorder, moving from sexual immorality in chapter 5 to lawsuits here, both symptoms of a proud, worldly church. Verses 1 to 6 confront the scandal of suing before unbelievers; verses 7 to 8 expose the deeper failure beneath the lawsuits; verses 9 to 11 widen to a warning and the gospel reversal, which then leads into the teaching on the body and immorality in verses 12 to 20. The repeated 'Do you not know' drives the argument.
context.historical: Roman Corinth was famously litigious, and its courts favoured the powerful, who used lawsuits to shame and dominate social rivals. For believers to drag one another before such courts imported this status-driven combat into the church and displayed its divisions before a watching, unbelieving public. Arbitration within a community was a known practice, which sharpens Paul's rebuke that they could not find one wise person to arbitrate.
context.canonical: The promise that the saints will judge the world and angels draws on Daniel 7:22 and is developed in Revelation 20:4. The vice list and the warning about inheriting the kingdom echo similar lists in Galatians 5 and elsewhere, and the threefold washed, sanctified, justified gathers up the New Testament's rich language for the whole of salvation applied to the believer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To shame the Corinthians out of a litigiousness that betrays the gospel before unbelievers, to teach that the saints' future destiny of judging the world should reshape how they handle present disputes, to warn soberly that unrepentant sin excludes from the kingdom, and to anchor their identity in the washing, sanctifying, and justifying work of Christ and the Spirit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The rebuke of lawsuits is prescriptive for the church: disputes among believers should be settled within the community, and it is better to suffer wrong than to shame Christ. The vice list describes the old life the readers have left; it is a warning against returning to it, and it must be read together with verse 11, which declares what God has made them.

debates.question: How should the vice list of verses 9 and 10, including its words about sexual sin, be understood and applied?

views.label: A sober warning against unrepentant sin
views.case: The list names patterns of settled, unrepentant behaviour that mark the life of those outside the kingdom; it warns professing believers not to be deceived into thinking such a life is compatible with inheriting the kingdom, while treating all the sins named, from greed and drunkenness to sexual sin, with equal seriousness.

views.label: A backdrop for grace, not a verdict of no hope
views.case: The whole point of the list is verse 11, such were some of you: these very sins do not place anyone beyond the reach of Christ, for the Corinthians themselves were washed, sanctified, and justified; the passage offers real hope and transformation, not condemnation without remedy.

debates.bearing: Read together, the two views guard both truth and grace: sin must be named honestly and not excused, yet no sinner who turns to Christ is beyond washing and justification; the passage is pastoral and hopeful, never a license to despair or to condemn.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's rebuke turns on a deliberate irony: the Corinthians run to the adikos, the unrighteous, for justice, when the saints themselves will one day krino, judge, the world and even angels alongside Christ. If so great a role awaits them, the everyday quarrels dividing them are trivial by comparison. He then presses the warning with kleronomeo, to inherit: the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom, which is an inheritance received by grace, not seized by the litigious. And the passage crowns its argument with dikaioo, justified: the Corinthians, once numbered among the unrighteous, were declared righteous in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of God, the verdict that moves them from the excluded to the heirs.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfils the promise that judgment would be given to the holy ones of the Most High in Daniel 7:22, realized as the saints reign and judge with Christ in Revelation 20:4.
The ritual washings and cleansing of the old covenant point forward to the true washing of regeneration and renewal by the Spirit, by which God makes sinners clean in Christ.

study.christ: Everything turns on the name of the Lord Jesus Christ. It is in His name and by the Spirit that the Corinthians were washed, sanctified, and justified, so that His person and finished work are the ground of their whole transformation. Christ is the Judge with whom the saints will judge the world and angels, and union with Him is what moves people from the roll of the unrighteous to the heirs of the kingdom. Even Paul's call to be wronged rather than to retaliate reflects the pattern of Christ, who when He was reviled did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to the One who judges justly.
study.god: God is perfectly just and will judge the world; the unrighteous, if unchanged, will not inherit His kingdom. Yet the same God takes the initiative to wash, sanctify, and justify sinners, making the unrighteous righteous through Christ and by His Spirit. The kingdom is His inheritance to give, and He gives it freely to those who are His in Christ. In verse 11 the whole Trinity is at work: the Father whose Spirit renews, the Son in whose name they are justified, and the Spirit by whom it is done.

crossReferences.note: Judgment given to the saints of the Most High, the background to the saints judging the world.
The saints reign and judge with Christ, fulfilling the destiny Paul invokes.
Do not repay evil for evil or avenge yourselves, matching the call to be wronged rather than retaliate.
The washing of regeneration and renewal by the Spirit, justified by grace as heirs, echoing washed, sanctified, justified.
A parallel warning that those who practise the works of the flesh will not inherit the kingdom of God.

study.oneStory: God is forming a people destined to share His righteous rule, judging the world and angels with Christ. The petty lawsuits of Corinth betray that high calling, trading heavenly destiny for courtroom victories before unbelievers. But the gospel reversal at the heart of the passage, washed, sanctified, justified, tells the whole Bible's story in miniature: God takes the unrighteous and makes them heirs of His kingdom through the Son and by the Spirit. From sinners once barred from the kingdom to saints who will one day judge the world, the story is the triumph of grace.

quiz.q: Why is Paul astonished that the Corinthians take their disputes to pagan courts?

options.text: Because the saints will one day judge the world and even angels.
options.note: Verses 2 and 3 ground the rebuke in the saints' future role as judges.

options.text: Because pagan courts always ruled unfairly.
options.note: Bias was real, but Paul's argument is the saints' own destiny to judge.

options.text: Because lawsuits were illegal for believers.
options.note: The issue is not legality but the shame and inconsistency of it.

options.text: Because Christians should never resolve disputes at all.
options.note: Paul wants disputes settled, but within the church, by the wise.

quiz.q: What does Paul say the lawsuits themselves already reveal?

options.text: That the Corinthians are thoroughly defeated already.
options.note: Verse 7 says having lawsuits at all is already a defeat.

options.text: That they simply need better lawyers.
options.note: The problem is spiritual defeat, not legal strategy.

options.text: That they are zealous for justice.
options.note: Their zeal is misdirected; the lawsuits are a failure, not a virtue.

options.text: That the church is spiritually healthy.
options.note: The lawsuits are a symptom of deep dysfunction.

quiz.q: What does Paul urge as better than suing a brother?

options.text: To rather be wronged or cheated than to shame the church.
options.note: Verse 7 asks, why not rather be wronged, why not rather be cheated.

options.text: To retaliate quietly outside the courts.
options.note: Retaliation is not the answer; Paul counsels willingness to suffer wrong.

options.text: To ignore all wrongdoing as if it did not happen.
options.note: The point is not indifference but willingness to bear loss for Christ.

options.text: To demand full repayment before forgiving.
options.note: This is the opposite of being willing to be wronged.

quiz.q: What sober warning does Paul give in verses 9 and 10?

options.text: The unrighteous who persist unrepentant will not inherit the kingdom of God.
options.note: Verses 9 and 10 warn that such a life excludes from the kingdom.

options.text: That some sins are too small for God to notice.
options.note: Paul takes every sin named seriously, not lightly.

options.text: That believers can sin freely without consequence.
options.note: The warning is precisely against presuming on grace.

options.text: That no one can ever be sure of the kingdom.
options.note: Verse 11 gives real assurance to those washed and justified.

quiz.q: What is the gospel reversal Paul declares in verse 11?

options.text: Such were some of you, but you were washed, sanctified, and justified in Christ and by the Spirit.
options.note: Verse 11 is the triumphant turn from the vice list to grace.

options.text: That they cleaned up their own lives by effort.
options.note: The change is God's work in Christ and by the Spirit, not self-reform.

options.text: That their past sins still define them.
options.note: Paul says such were some of you, past tense; they have been changed.

options.text: That only some of the sins could be forgiven.
options.note: The whole list is answered by the washing and justifying grace of God.

questions: Paul says the saints will judge the world and angels. How might remembering your eternal destiny reshape the way you handle a present conflict?
Better to be wronged than to shame Christ before outsiders. Is there a dispute where you are being called to absorb loss rather than fight to win?
The lawsuits themselves were already a defeat. Where might winning an argument actually mean losing something far more important?
The vice list describes a life outside the kingdom, yet such were some of you. How does that phrase give you hope for yourself or for someone you have given up on?
You were washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit. Which of those three words do you most need to hear over your life today?
Paul assumes the church should be able to resolve its own disputes wisely. What would it take for your community to become a place where that is true?

translationNotes.note: Before the unrighteous renders the same root, adikos, used in verse 9 for those who will not inherit the kingdom; Paul highlights the irony of seeking justice from the unrighteous.

The phrase rendered men who submit to or perform homosexual acts translates two Greek terms describing both partners in male same-sex activity; the list treats this sin alongside greed, theft, and drunkenness, all named with equal seriousness and all answered by the grace of verse 11.

Washed, sanctified, justified are three complementary pictures of one saving work, done in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of God, and are best read as God's decisive act, not a gradual self-improvement.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 6:1-11; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas or semicolons per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
